title: Federal Grant Fight Snarls Port of Galveston Automation Project
body:
A $240 million federal infrastructure grant awarded to modernize cargo handling at the Port of Galveston is mired in a dispute between the port authority, logistics operator Stelmark Freight, and the International Longshoremen's Association, sources familiar with the matter said.

The Department of Transportation awarded the grant in January under its Ports of the Future program. But the port authority's contract with Stelmark, which would deploy automated stacking cranes and vehicle guidance systems, has drawn fierce opposition from the ILA's Gulf Coast district, which represents roughly 900 dockworkers at the facility.

ILA regional director Clyde Okafor said the contract, as written, would eliminate at least 200 positions within three years. "This is public money being used to put our members out of work," he said at a press conference Wednesday.

Stelmark, whose regional director declined to comment on record, has previously deployed similar systems at ports in Savannah and Long Beach. The company's chief operating officer, Miriam Solano, gave a presentation to the port commission last month in which she projected the automation would reduce cargo dwell time by 30 percent.

The port authority has asked for a 60-day mediation period. A federal DOT spokesperson said the grant terms do not require a specific vendor but do mandate that any system "demonstrate material throughput improvement."
